summary: Drew DeVault calls out the Hyprland Wayland compositor community for fostering a toxic, hateful environment despite being an exciting technical project. He documents specific incidents including moderators harassing a transgender user by editing their pronouns, community tolerance of eugenics endorsements, and leadership dismissing concerns as jokes. DeVault recounts his private outreach to project lead Vaxry, which yielded minimal improvement, and offers constructive advice to leadership, community members, and victims. He frames the issue as a failure of leadership culture, drawing on his own past regrets about being mean while young and productive.
key_points: Hyprland's community tolerates hate speech and bullying under the guise of not taking things 'too seriously', with moderation only intervening when hate becomes disruptive rather than when it's harmful

The toxicity is top-down: moderators actively participated in harassing a trans user, and project leader Vaxry dismisses the severity by comparing himself to Terry Davis and mocking moderated communities
DeVault attempted private resolution first, but despite Vaxry's own poll showing 40% of members experienced harassment, meaningful change did not follow
The post argues that open source communities have a responsibility to be welcoming to everyone, and that exciting technical work doesn't excuse hostile community culture
DeVault offers empathy alongside criticism, sharing his own experience of being 'young, smart, productive, and mean' as a way to model growth
money_quotes: Imagine a high school boys' locker room come to life on Discord and GitHub and you'll get an idea of what it's like.
Part of what makes open source software great is that it's great for everyone.
I remember being young, smart, productive… and mean. I did some cool stuff, but I deeply regret the way I treated people.
It wasn't really my fault – I was a product of my environment – but it was my responsibility.
What motivates us to build and give away free software if not bringing joy to ourselves and others?
Can we be proud of a community which brings more suffering into the world?
When Vaxry is older and wiser, I want him to look back on the project and community that he's built with pride and joy, not with regret and shame.
Participate in the community as you think it should be, not as it necessarily is, and change will follow.
key_insight: Technical excellence cannot redeem a community that tolerates hate, and leadership bears direct responsibility for the culture they allow to flourish.
